title: Approximability limits for bounded-degree max-LINSAT and implications for decoded quantum interferometry
authors: Maximilian J. Kramer, Carsten Schubert, Jens Eisert
abstract: For general max-k-XORSAT with $k \geq 3$, no polynomial-time algorithm can do substantially better than random guessing on worst-case instances unless $\mathsf{P} = \mathsf{NP}$: approximating beyond the random-assignment value of $1/2$ is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard. The picture changes when each variable appears in at most $D$ constraints. In that bounded-degree setting, polynomial-time algorithms can provably beat the random baseline by an additive amount of order $1/\sqrt{D}$. For Boolean instances, this scaling is known to be optimal: the matching hardness result is due to Trevisan, while the corresponding algorithmic guarantee was established by Barak et al. Whether the same holds over general finite fields, and what it implies for quantum algorithms, has not been established. We make this connection explicit and extend the hardness to max-E$k$-LINSAT$(q,r)$ with bounded degree $D$ and over arbitrary finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, proving that it is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard to exceed $r/q + \mathcal{O}_{q,r}(1/\sqrt{D})$. These results provide the complexity-theoretic benchmark for the bounded-degree instances targeted by decoded quantum interferometry (DQI), QAOA, and classical heuristics. Any quantum advantage on bounded-degree instances is therefore confined to the constant prefactor. We further show that in the context of DQI and on $(k,D)$-regular instances, this prefactor is sensitive to the nature of the decoder: DQI with classical decoders faces an information-theoretic $1/\sqrt{D \log D}$ barrier that prevents it from matching the hardness scaling, while DQI with quantum decoders is compatible with the $1/\sqrt{D}$ scaling -- identifying quantum decoding as the key ingredient for matching the complexity-theoretic scaling with DQI.
summary: This work establishes the worst-case inapproximability limits for solving sparse, bounded-degree constraint satisfaction problems (max-LINSAT). By connecting complexity theory to quantum decoding bounds, it shows that achieving a certain approximation ratio requires overcoming an $O(1/\sqrt{D})$ barrier. This result critically identifies quantum decoding as necessary to match the theoretical hardness scaling in physical applications like DQI.
main_problem: The paper characterizes the best possible approximation ratios for solving bounded-degree maximum linear constraint satisfaction problems (max-LINSAT) over finite fields.
main_result: It proves that approximating these worst-case instances is NP-hard beyond a threshold of $r/q + O(1/\sqrt{D})$, establishing a complexity benchmark for quantum algorithms like DQI.
method: The analysis combines established results from computational complexity theory (PCP theorems, degree reduction) with information-theoretic bounds derived from decoding quantum interferometry principles.
model_or_system: The theoretical model is the max-LINSAT problem over finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, which serves as a mathematical benchmark for physical systems targeted by decoded quantum interferometry (DQI).
key_observables: Approximation ratio ($\alpha$), scaling factor $1/\sqrt{D}$, and the gap between classical vs. quantum decoding bounds.
important_parameters: Variable degree ($D$), constraint arity ($k$), finite field size ($q$), and acceptance set size ($r$).
main_assumptions:
The analysis assumes $	ext{NP} 
ot\subseteq 	ext{BPP}$ (or related complexity separations) to establish the hardness results.
figures_summary: Figure 2 illustrates the approximability landscape for max-Ek-LINSAT(q,r), comparing classical and quantum decoding radii against the established $1/\sqrt{D}$ hardness scaling.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing general inapproximability results for XORSAT, then extending these techniques via degree reduction to the more general max-LINSAT(q,r), culminating in a detailed comparison of quantum vs. classical decoding performance against complexity theory.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous theoretical benchmark showing that any potential quantum advantage on structured optimization problems like DQI must be confined to improving constant prefactors rather than changing the fundamental scaling law dictated by computational hardness.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
